title: Workaholism As Polite Rage
dek: You call it ambition, but your body knows the truth. The relentless drive to produce, the inability to rest, the 3 AM emails—this is not dedication. This is fury, neatly packaged and socially rewarded, burning through your adrenal glands and locking your jaw.
bodyMarkdown:
## The Midnight Buzz and the Clenched Jaw

You are awake again. It is 3:14 AM, and the blue light of your phone illuminates the ceiling of your bedroom. Your thumb scrolls through emails you have already read, Slack messages you have already answered, project timelines that do not need your attention until the sun comes up. You tell yourself you are just getting ahead of the day. You tell yourself this is what it takes to succeed, to be responsible, to hold it all together. But pay attention to your body right now. Notice the rigid line of your masseter muscle, clamped down so hard your teeth ache. Notice the shallow, rapid flutter of your breath, barely moving past your collarbones. Notice the tight, hot knot sitting just below your sternum, right in the center of your diaphragm. This is not the physiology of dedication. This is the physiology of a fight-or-flight response that has nowhere to go. You are not working because you love it. You are working because if you stop, you will have to feel the massive, unexpressed rage simmering just beneath your skin.

We are taught that anger is loud. We are taught that fury looks like screaming, throwing things, losing control. For women, especially, we are taught that anger is ugly, dangerous, and deeply unlovable. So we learn to swallow it. We learn to push it down into the dark, quiet corners of our bodies. But energy does not simply disappear because you refuse to acknowledge it. The body knows. The jaw remembers. The fury you swallowed when you were talked over in that meeting, the rage you pushed down when your boundaries were violated, the deep, primal anger at a world that demands your constant labor and offers so little in return—it all has to go somewhere. And for many of us, it goes into the hustle. Workaholism is the most socially acceptable form of polite rage. It is a way to burn off the adrenaline and cortisol flooding your system without ever having to admit you are angry. You channel the aggressive, destructive energy of your fury into building, producing, achieving. You become a machine, because machines do not feel rage. But you are not a machine. You are a living, breathing organism, and this polite rage is slowly tearing you apart from the inside out.

The physical toll of this relentless, anger-fueled drive is staggering. Your adrenal glands, those tiny, walnut-shaped organs sitting on top of your kidneys, are pumping out stress hormones in a desperate attempt to keep you moving. Your sympathetic nervous system is locked in the "on" position, treating every unanswered email like a mortal threat. The fascia wrapping around your muscles becomes thick and rigid, armor plating your body against a danger that is coming from inside the house. You feel it in the burning ache of your trapezius muscles, the stiff, unyielding tension in your levator scapulae. Your body is bracing for an impact that never comes, exhausting its resources in a perpetual state of red alert. This is not pathology, this is biology. Your body is doing exactly what it was designed to do when faced with a threat. The problem is that the threat is your own unacknowledged anger, and you cannot outwork it.

## The Biology of Swallowed Fury

To understand workaholism as polite rage, you have to understand how anger moves through the body. In Traditional Chinese Medicine, anger is associated with the Liver meridian. The Liver is responsible for the smooth flow of Qi, or vital energy, throughout the body. When anger is expressed healthily, it moves up and out, a quick, hot burst of energy that clears the air and restores boundaries. But when anger is suppressed, when it is swallowed and denied, it stagnates. The Liver Qi becomes blocked, creating a pressure cooker effect inside your body. This stagnant energy generates heat, which rises and gets trapped in the upper body—the tight chest, the clenched jaw, the headaches, the red, burning eyes of the exhausted overworker.

Western biology tells a similar story, though it uses different language. When you experience anger, your amygdala signals the hypothalamus, which triggers the release of adrenaline and noradrenaline. Your heart rate increases, your blood pressure rises, and blood is shunted away from your digestive organs and toward your large muscle groups, preparing you to fight or flee. This is a massive mobilization of energy. But when you are sitting at a desk, staring at a screen, you cannot fight and you cannot flee. You have to sit still and be professional. So you channel that mobilized energy into your work. You type harder, you think faster, you push yourself further. You use the physiological arousal of anger to fuel your productivity.

But the body was not designed to sustain this level of arousal indefinitely. The constant flood of stress hormones begins to wear down your system. The psoas muscle, the deep core muscle that connects your spine to your legs and is intimately involved in the fight-or-flight response, becomes chronically tight, pulling your pelvis out of alignment and causing lower back pain. Your digestion suffers because your body is constantly diverting resources away from your gut. You develop acid reflux, irritable bowel syndrome, or chronic constipation. Your immune system becomes compromised, leaving you vulnerable to every cold and flu that passes through the office. You are literally working yourself to death, and you are doing it because you are too terrified to stop and feel the rage that is driving you.

## Gabor Maté and the Addiction to Doing

Dr. Gabor Maté, a physician and expert on addiction and trauma, has written extensively about how our society normalizes and even rewards certain forms of addiction, particularly workaholism. Maté argues that all addiction is an attempt to escape pain. It is a desperate bid to soothe a dysregulated nervous system and avoid feeling the deep, underlying wounds that we carry. When we look at workaholism through this lens, it becomes clear that the relentless drive to produce is not about ambition or dedication. It is about avoidance.

Maté points out that the workaholic is often running from a profound sense of inadequacy, a deep-seated belief that they are not enough just as they are. This belief is often rooted in early childhood experiences where love and approval were conditional, tied to performance and achievement. The anger that stems from this conditional love—the rage at not being seen, valued, and loved for who you are, rather than what you can do—is too dangerous for a child to express. So it gets buried. And as an adult, you continue the pattern. You work obsessively to prove your worth, to earn the love and safety you were denied. The work becomes a frantic, exhausting attempt to outrun the pain and the rage.

But the body keeps the score, as Bessel van der Kolk famously noted. The anger you are trying to outrun is living in your tissues. It is in the tight, restricted movement of your diaphragm, preventing you from taking a full, deep breath. It is in the chronic inflammation coursing through your bloodstream. Maté's work reminds us that we cannot heal this by simply working less or taking a vacation. We have to turn toward the pain. We have to stop running, sit still, and allow the suppressed emotions to rise to the surface. We have to feel the rage. This is terrifying work, which is why we avoid it so fiercely. It is much easier to answer another email, to take on another project, to stay at the office until midnight. But the cost of this avoidance is your life. You are sacrificing your vitality, your health, and your capacity for true connection on the altar of productivity.

## The Somatic Cost of the Hustle

Let us look closer at the specific, somatic reality of this polite rage. When you are caught in the grip of workaholism, your body is in a state of chronic contraction. You are literally bracing yourself against the world. Pay attention to your shoulders right now. Are they creeping up toward your ears? This is the action of the upper trapezius and the levator scapulae, muscles that contract instinctively to protect the vulnerable structures of the neck when we feel threatened. In the workaholic body, these muscles are constantly engaged, creating a hard, painful ridge of tension across the top of the back.

Now bring your attention to your belly. Is it soft and relaxed, or is it pulled in tight? The chronic contraction of the abdominal muscles is a classic sign of suppressed emotion, particularly anger and fear. When we tighten our bellies, we restrict the movement of the diaphragm, the large, dome-shaped muscle that separates the chest cavity from the abdominal cavity. A restricted diaphragm means shallow, chest-breathing, which signals to the nervous system that we are in danger, perpetuating the cycle of anxiety and arousal. This shallow breathing also means that the lower lobes of the lungs, where the most efficient oxygen exchange occurs, are not being fully utilized. You are literally starving your body of oxygen in order to keep your emotions locked down.

And then there is the jaw. The masseter muscle, the primary muscle of chewing, is one of the strongest muscles in the human body relative to its size. It is also a primary repository for unexpressed anger. When we want to scream, bite, or yell, but we force ourselves to remain silent and polite, the masseter clamps down. This chronic clenching leads to temporomandibular joint (TMJ) dysfunction, tension headaches, and cracked teeth. Your dentist tells you to wear a nightguard, but a piece of plastic cannot resolve the fury that is driving your jaw to grind your teeth to dust while you sleep. The body is desperately trying to process the energy of the rage, using the only avenues available to it.

## Resmaa Menakem and the Lineage of Trapped Energy

To fully grasp the depth of this polite rage, we must look beyond our individual experiences and consider the collective and ancestral trauma we carry. Resmaa Menakem, a somatic therapist and author of *My Grandmother's Hands*, writes profoundly about how trauma and unexpressed energy are passed down through generations, living in our bodies as a "dirty pain." Menakem's work focuses primarily on racialized trauma, but his insights into how the body stores and transmits unresolved energy are crucial for understanding the somatic reality of workaholism.

Menakem teaches that when a trauma or a massive surge of energy (like rage) is not allowed to complete its cycle and discharge from the nervous system, it gets stuck in the body. This stuck energy creates a somatic contraction, a physical bracing that becomes our default state of being. Over time, this contraction feels normal. We forget what it feels like to be relaxed, open, and undefended. For many of us, the polite rage of workaholism is not just our own; it is the inherited rage of mothers and grandmothers who were denied agency, who were forced into subservience, who had to swallow their fury to survive. We carry their unexpressed anger in our own tissues, and we channel it into the only socially acceptable outlet we have: relentless productivity.

Menakem emphasizes that we cannot think our way out of this somatic contraction. We cannot simply decide to stop being workaholics. We have to work with the body. We have to slowly, gently build the capacity of our nervous systems to tolerate the intense sensations of the trapped energy. We have to learn to stay present with the heat, the vibration, the urge to move, without immediately channeling it into work or distraction. This is the practice of somatic abolitionism—dismantling the internalized structures of oppression and trauma that live in our flesh. When we begin to release the polite rage, we are not just healing ourselves; we are breaking a generational chain of suppressed fury.

## Herbal Allies for the Enraged and Exhausted

When the body has been running on the high-octane fuel of polite rage for years, you cannot simply hit the brakes and expect everything to be fine. The nervous system is frayed, the adrenal glands are depleted, and the Liver Qi is severely stagnated. We need serious, grounded medicine to support the body in this transition. This is where the plants come in. Herbs are not just biochemical compounds; they are somatic guides, teaching the body how to shift out of chronic contraction and into a state of flow and repair.

First, we must address the exhausted nervous system. Nervines are a class of herbs that specifically nourish and calm the nervous system. Skullcap (*Scutellaria lateriflora*) is a profound ally for the workaholic. It is specific for the person who cannot turn their brain off, whose mind is constantly spinning with to-do lists and anxieties. Skullcap helps to quiet the mental chatter and release the physical tension that accompanies it, particularly in the neck and shoulders. It teaches the body that it is safe to rest. You might consider a strong tincture of Skullcap, perhaps [Gaia Herbs Sound Sleep](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B0036THNRA?tag=spankyspinola-20), taken in the evening to help transition from the hustle of the day into the quiet of the night.

Next, we must support the adrenal glands, which have been bearing the brunt of the polite rage. Adaptogens are herbs that help the body adapt to stress and restore balance to the endocrine system. Ashwagandha (*Withania somnifera*) is a deeply restorative adaptogen, particularly suited for the exhausted, wired-and-tired presentation of the chronic overworker. It helps to lower cortisol levels, improve sleep quality, and rebuild the body's deep reserves of energy. It is a heavy, grounding root that pulls the frantic, upward-rising energy of the workaholic back down into the earth.

Finally, we must address the stagnant Liver Qi, the trapped anger itself. In TCM, bitter herbs are used to clear heat and move stagnation in the Liver. Milk Thistle (*Silybum marianum*) is a powerful liver protectant and restorative. It helps the liver process and eliminate toxins, both physical and emotional. Dandelion root (*Taraxacum officinale*) is another excellent liver tonic, gently stimulating the flow of bile and helping to clear the heat of unexpressed frustration. By supporting the physical organ of the liver, we support the energetic movement of the anger, allowing it to flow up and out rather than turning inward and fueling the relentless drive to work.

## The Embodied Practice: Releasing the Jaw and the Psoas

Reading about polite rage is not enough. You have to feel it, and you have to give it a way to move. This practice is designed to gently access the trapped energy in the jaw and the psoas, two of the primary holding sites for unexpressed anger. Do this practice in a quiet space where you will not be interrupted.

1. **The Setup:** Lie on your back on the floor, with your knees bent and your feet flat on the ground, hip-width apart. Place your hands on your belly. Close your eyes and take three slow, deep breaths, allowing your belly to rise into your hands on the inhale and fall on the exhale.
2. **Locating the Tension:** Bring your awareness to your jaw. Notice the sensation of the masseter muscle. Is it tight? Does it feel hard or dense? Do not try to change it yet; just observe it. Now bring your awareness to your deep core, the area just inside your hip bones. This is the territory of the psoas. Notice any gripping, holding, or numbness in this area.
3. **The Jaw Release:** Slowly open your mouth as wide as you comfortably can. Hold it open for a count of three, feeling the stretch in the masseter. Now, slowly close your mouth, but do not let your teeth touch. Keep a small space between your upper and lower teeth. Gently slide your lower jaw to the right, hold for a breath, then slide it to the left, hold for a breath. Return to center. Repeat this sequence three times. As you do this, imagine the tight, hot energy of the polite rage beginning to soften and dissipate.
4. **The Psoas Release:** With your feet flat on the floor, gently press your lower back into the ground, tilting your pelvis slightly upward. Hold for a breath, then release, allowing the natural curve of your lower back to return. Now, slowly let your right knee fall out to the side, opening the hip. Only go as far as is comfortable. Hold for three breaths, breathing directly into the sensation in the right side of your pelvis. Slowly bring the knee back to center. Repeat on the left side.
5. **The Integration:** Bring both knees back to center. Place one hand on your heart and one hand on your belly. Allow yourself to feel whatever emotions are rising to the surface. You may feel a sudden surge of heat, a desire to cry, or a subtle trembling in your muscles. This is the trapped energy beginning to move. Do not judge it, and do not try to stop it. Just breathe and let the body do what it needs to do. Stay here for at least five minutes, simply witnessing the unwinding of the polite rage.

## FAQ: Understanding and Unwinding Polite Rage

**Q: I love my job and I am very successful. Are you saying my ambition is just a trauma response?**

**A:** Not necessarily. Ambition and a desire to create, build, and contribute are natural, healthy human impulses. The distinction lies in the somatic experience of the work. Healthy ambition feels expansive, joyful, and sustainable. It is fueled by inspiration and a sense of purpose. Polite rage, on the other hand, feels compulsive, frantic, and exhausting. It is fueled by a fear of stopping, a need to prove your worth, and a deep, underlying tension in the body. If you cannot take a week off without your nervous system going into a panic, your ambition is likely masking something else. The body knows the difference between joyful creation and desperate avoidance.

**Q: If I stop working so hard, I am afraid I will lose everything I have built. How do I balance healing with the reality of needing to make a living?**

**A:** This is a very real and valid fear. We live in a capitalist system that demands our constant labor and punishes rest. Healing polite rage does not mean you quit your job and move to the woods (unless you want to). It means changing your relationship to the work. It means learning to work from a place of grounded presence rather than frantic avoidance. As you begin to release the trapped anger and regulate your nervous system, you will find that you can actually be more effective and focused in your work, because you are no longer wasting massive amounts of energy managing the internal pressure cooker. You start setting boundaries, not as a mental exercise, but as a somatic necessity. You stop saying yes when your body is screaming no.

**Q: I tried the embodied practice and I just felt numb. Does that mean it is not working?**

**A:** Numbness is a very common and completely normal response. When the body has been holding onto intense, overwhelming energy for a long time, its primary defense mechanism is to shut down the sensation entirely. Numbness is not an absence of feeling; it is a profound, active suppression of feeling. If you feel numb, simply notice the numbness. Where is it located? Does it have a texture or a temperature? Be curious about it. Do not force yourself to feel something else. Healing happens at the speed of safety. Your body needs to know that it is safe to thaw out before it will allow the deeper sensations of the polite rage to surface. Keep practicing, gently and consistently, and the numbness will eventually begin to shift.

**Q: Can I just take the herbs you mentioned and keep working the way I am?**

**A:** You can, but it will be like pouring water into a bucket with a hole in it. Herbs are powerful allies, but they are not magic bullets that can override the consequences of your daily choices. If you take Skullcap to calm your nervous system but continue to work 80-hour weeks and ignore your body's signals, the herbs will only provide temporary, superficial relief. The true medicine is the combination of the herbal support and the somatic unwinding. The herbs give your body the resources it needs to do the deep work of releasing the polite rage, but you still have to do the work. You have to be willing to feel the fury, to set the boundaries, and to reclaim your right to rest.
